critique.summary: The article discusses the complexities and frustrations of developing native Windows applications in 2024, highlighting the fragmented and outdated ecosystem of Windows UI frameworks and deployment options. The author shares personal experience building a simple utility app and critiques Microsoft's inconsistent support and tooling, ultimately suggesting that web technologies like Electron or Tauri are more practical choices for modern Windows app development.
mainPoints: Windows native app development has a long history of evolving UI frameworks, from Win32 to MFC, WinForms, WPF, WinRT, UWP, and now WinUI 3, each with compatibility and capability gaps.

Developers face difficult choices between C++, .NET framework-dependent deployment, and .NET AOT, with none offering an ideal experience due to issues like outdated preinstalled .NET versions and large binary sizes.
Key app features such as global keyboard shortcuts, tray icons, and certain window behaviors require falling back to older Win32 APIs via P/Invoke, complicating development.
Microsoft's packaging and deployment system (MSIX) is cumbersome and costly for independent developers, especially due to expensive code signing certificates and restrictive store policies.

The C# language and interop tooling (e.g., CsWin32) have limitations that hinder seamless integration with Windows APIs, and modern language features to reduce boilerplate in UI binding remain lacking.

Microsoft appears to deprioritize native Windows app development, with many first-party apps using web technologies and community efforts shifting to third-party cross-platform frameworks or Electron.
Despite some recent improvements in the Windows App SDK and WinUI 3, the ecosystem remains fragmented and frustrating, leading many developers to prefer web-based solutions.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are largely accurate and reflect the current state of Windows native app development as confirmed by external sources including Microsoft documentation and developer community feedback. The historical overview of Windows UI frameworks aligns with documented timelines. The challenges with .NET versioning, MSIX packaging, and P/Invoke reliance are well-known issues in the developer community. The critique of C# language limitations and tooling such as CsWin32 is supported by ongoing discussions among Windows developers. The author's personal experience and opinions are consistent with broader industry observations about the decline of native Windows app development in favor of web technologies like Electron. However, some subjective assessments, such as the quality of UI frameworks or Microsoft's priorities, reflect personal perspective rather than objective fact.
considerations: The article does not deeply explore the benefits of native Windows development for performance-critical or specialized applications where native code is still essential.
It omits discussion of recent Microsoft initiatives like .NET 7/8 improvements and community-driven projects that might address some of the tooling and deployment challenges.
The security and sandboxing advantages of UWP and WinRT, despite their limitations, are not fully examined.
The article focuses on desktop app development and does not address Windows development for other device types like Xbox or HoloLens, which may have different frameworks and priorities.
The cost and complexity of code signing certificates might vary by region and organization size, which could affect developer experience differently.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the complexity and fragmentation of Windows native app development, with many commenters advocating for sticking to Win32 due to its stability and backward compatibility. Others highlight alternative frameworks like Qt, Avalonia, and Electron, while some emphasize the challenges of deployment and code signing on Windows.

comments.author: userbinator
comments.text:
I agree with all the comments here saying "stick with Win32" --- this is "a mess" that you can easily avoid.

Speaking as a long-time Win32 programmer, the requirements for your app are doable in a few KB (yes, kilobytes --- my vague estimate is less than 8KB) standalone executable. This is how I arrived at that:

Enumerating the machine’s displays and their bounds

A few API calls. Probably a few hundred bytes.

Placing borderless, titlebar-less, non-activating black windows

Creating non-functional windows is trivial. Another few hundred bytes at most.

Intercepting a global keyboard shortcut

A few dozen bytes to call SetWindowsHookEx.

Optionally running at startup

Write to the appropriate registry key. A few hundred bytes.

Storing some persistent settings

Ditto. Another few hundred bytes. You can use a .ini file too, for around the same size.

Displaying a tray icon with a few menu items

Most of this size of this will be the icon itself - a few kilobytes; the next biggest contributor will be text strings; and the rest is accomplished with a few hundred bytes of API calls.

Add another few hundred bytes of (not much) logic, round up to a kilobyte and add maybe another for general overhead.

But, in 2026, writing a greenfield application in a memory-unsafe language like C++ is a crime.

Don't be swayed by the propaganda. Especially if your application has essentially no untrusted input.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and practical breakdown of Win32 API usage and argues convincingly for its continued relevance, especially for simple, trusted applications.

comments.author: cv5005
comments.text:
I'm an embedded programmer who occassionally needs to write various windows programs to interface with embedded devices (usually via serial port or usb), and I find it a breeze to write native gui programs in pure win32 and c++.

Recently had to add a new feature to and old program that was last updated in the XP era and two things to note:

1. The program did not need to be updated to run on Vista, 7, 10 and 11, shit just kept working throughout the years.

2. I loaded the project into Visual Studio 2022, it converted from VC6 and compiled without problems, added the feature, shipped a new .exe to the customer, and it just worked.

What other platform has that backwards and forwards compatibility success story?
comments.analysis: This comment highlights the strong backward compatibility of Win32 applications, supported by personal experience spanning multiple Windows versions.

comments.author: pjmlp
comments.text:
Again, unless you have existing Windows 8/10 applications that were written against WinRT, UAP or UWP[0], that make use of WinUI 2.0, forget about touching anything related to WinUI 3.0 or WinAppSDK, stay away from the marketing.

Exception being the few APIs that have been introduced in Win32 that instead of COM, actually depend on WinRT like the new MIDI 2.0 or Windows ML.

Keep using Win32, MFC (yes it is in a better state than WinUI 3.0 with C++), WinForms, WPF, if using Microsoft only tooling.

Otherwise, Qt, VCL, Firemonkey, Avalonia, Uno, ImGUI,....

They were even forced to revamp WPF status at BUILD 2024, given how bad WinUI 3.0 was back then, and it isn't if it got any better, apparently it is in the process of being open sourced, to see if the community can take over the mess a $4 trillion valued company cannot fix.

Really, stay away from WinUI, unless you're a Microsoft employee on the Windows team without any other option.

[0] - Can explain by the nth time the differences, if one feels like it.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced view on the state of modern Windows UI frameworks, advising caution and suggesting alternatives, reflecting deep knowledge of the ecosystem.

comments.author: iamcalledrob
comments.text:
The author is right, it's really such a mess.

The lessons I've learnt building and shipping a few a Windows apps at scale are basically:

(1) Learn Win32 and use those ancient APIs if possible, they're extraordinarily stable and you'll probably need to reach for them anyway. They're not that scary.

(2) Don't use any Microsoft-owned UI toolkit, you'll get burnt. Literally anything is better. Ideally choose a toolkit that doesn't prevent layering in Win32 tweaks on top, otherwise you'll end up hitting cases the toolkit developers didn't think of and you can't fix. You're going to need a custom WindowProc eventually. You need to have access to the underlying Win32 window lifecycle and handles.
comments.analysis: This comment reinforces the article's main points, emphasizing the stability of Win32 and the pitfalls of newer Microsoft UI toolkits, based on practical development experience.

comments.author: drnick1
comments.text:
> But, in 2026, writing a greenfield application in a memory-unsafe language like C++ is a crime.

I disagree, the GUI layer is far from behind a safety critical component, and C++ is a battle-tested choice for everything from GUI, videos games, to industrial applications. If C++ is safe enough to control airplanes and nuclear reactors when used well, it is certainly safe enough for something as trivial a GUI.

The article also fails to mention frameworks like Qt, arguably the best way to write GUI apps in 2026. Qt is native (C++), has built-in memory safety features (but no GC), and is cross-platform.
comments.analysis: This comment challenges the notion that using C++ is unsafe for GUI development and points out Qt as a strong cross-platform alternative, adding valuable perspective.

comments.author: rahimnathwani
comments.text:
It had been many years since I last developed a desktop app. A couple of weeks ago I used Tauri to create a simple app for Windows and Mac. Developing the app was easy with Claude. Building the app for different platforms and architectures was easy with GitHub Actions.

But after I had the msi and dmg files, my non-techy colleagues couldn't install the apps because they weren't signed. The workaround for Mac was fine (remove the quarantine attribute on the installer) but for Windows my colleague had to disable Smart App Control (SAC), which cannot be re-enabled without re-installing Windows.

I get the point of these protections, but the difficulty of getting past them surprised me. I thought that on Mac you should just go to settings -> security and click 'Allow Anyway'. And that on Windows you'd get a GUI warning that would need admin privileges to get past. But MacOS needed a terminal command, and Windows needed a control panel setting change.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a real-world example of deployment challenges on Windows and Mac, particularly around code signing and security controls.

comments.author: ashwinnair99
comments.text: It has been a mess for 15 years and Microsoft keeps making it worse by adding new frameworks without retiring the old ones. Win32, WPF, WinUI, MAUI. Nobody knows which one to pick.
comments.analysis: This comment captures the frustration many developers feel about the fragmented Windows app development landscape and the lack of clear direction from Microsoft.

comments.author: PaulKeeble
comments.text: Most of the desktop applications I have wrote over the years have been in other languages like Java and Go as I have wanted them to mostly be cross platform. In these cases I have always used the Software UI, which in Java is Swing and in Go is Fyne. These are usually reasonably fast, don't necessarily look native depending on how its themed but ultimately fit the language better than trying to bridge to Win32 or GTK/QT.
comments.analysis: This comment offers an alternative viewpoint focusing on cross-platform development with languages like Java and Go, highlighting the trade-offs in native UI development.

comments.author: bigstrat2003
comments.text:
The answer to your question of "why not Electron" at the end is: because then your app will suck. You've laid out the reasons why native apps are harder to make, but the reality is that Electron trades your ease of development for the user having a crappy experience. If you care about producing a good product, then you have to suck it up and make the native app even if it is harder.

Also, I think C# is miles better than TypeScript, but that's just my preference.
comments.analysis: This comment argues strongly against Electron for production apps due to performance and user experience issues, advocating for native development despite the difficulties.

comments.author: Kwpolska
comments.text:
> However, for no reason I can understand, Microsoft has decided that even the latest versions of Windows 11 only get .NET 4.8.1 preinstalled.

.NET has new releases every year, supported for 2 or 3 years. That’s not really compatible with Windows release cycles. Also, if Windows 11 25H2 shipped .NET 8, and now Windows 11 26H2 would ship .NET 10, apps which depend on version 8 might break. Easier to just think of .NET as a runtime like Java or Python.

---

Regarding tray icons, 1Password, Signal, and Discord are all Electron apps, so they are using Chrome’s UI toolkit, and its menu component.

Myself, I’m happy with WPF. Starting with .NET 9, it comes with a really good WinUI-style theme.
comments.analysis: This comment clarifies the challenges around .NET runtime updates in Windows and shares optimism about improvements in newer .NET versions.
